name: Scrum team ceremony calendar for 2-week sprints
description: Design a complete ceremony calendar for a Scrum team of 7 developers, 1 PO, and 1 Scrum Master running 2-week sprints. Include all standard ceremonies with timing, duration, and recurrence rules.
input: We're a Scrum team with 7 developers, a Product Owner, and a Scrum Master. We run 2-week sprints starting on Mondays. We need a complete ceremony calendar. We're all co-located in the same office, working 9am-5pm.
expected_behavior: Produces a full ceremony calendar covering standup, planning, review, retro, and refinement
Standup is daily, 15 minutes max, scheduled at a consistent time
Sprint planning is on day 1 of the sprint, 2-4 hours
Sprint review is on the last day of the sprint, 1-2 hours
Sprint retrospective follows the review or is scheduled shortly after, 1-1.5 hours
Refinement sessions are mid-sprint, 1-2 hours per week
Ceremony load does not exceed 20% of total sprint hours
No day has more than 2 hours of ceremonies except sprint boundaries

name: Distributed team ceremonies across 3 timezones
description: Design ceremony schedule for a distributed team spanning US-East (UTC-5), Central Europe (UTC+1), and India (UTC+5:30) with fair overlap windows and async accommodations.
input: Our team is distributed across 3 timezones: 4 people in New York (UTC-5), 3 people in Berlin (UTC+1), and 3 people in Bangalore (UTC+5:30). We need a ceremony design that doesn't always disadvantage the same timezone. We run 2-week sprints.
expected_behavior: Identifies the overlap window between all 3 timezones (approximately 9:30-11:00 AM UTC+1)
Schedules critical ceremonies (planning, review, retro) within overlap windows
Rotates standup times across timezones or proposes async standup alternatives
Includes async pre-work to reduce synchronous meeting time
Provides remote facilitation adaptations for each ceremony
Addresses equalization strategies so no timezone is consistently disadvantaged
Recommends tools for async collaboration (digital whiteboards, async video updates)
Total synchronous ceremony time is minimized through async-first design

name: Retrospective format for team with retro fatigue
description: Recommend and design retrospective formats for a team that has been using the same Start-Stop-Continue format for 8 months and reports declining engagement and repetitive action items.
input: Our team has been doing Start-Stop-Continue retros for 8 months. Attendance has dropped from 100% to 70%. People say retros feel repetitive and the same issues keep coming up. Last 3 retros produced zero new action items. The team is 9 people, remote-first. Help us fix our retros.
expected_behavior: Diagnoses retro fatigue with specific evidence from the input (8 months same format, declining attendance, zero action items)
Recommends 3-4 alternative retro formats (e.g., Sailboat, 4Ls, Timeline, Appreciation Radar)
Provides a rotation schedule for formats to prevent future fatigue
Addresses the root cause of recurring issues: action item tracking and accountability
Includes facilitation techniques to re-engage disengaged team members
Proposes a mechanism to track action item completion between retros
Adapts all recommendations for remote-first execution
Suggests a retro-about-the-retro to let the team co-design their ceremony

name: Ceremony audit for 45-minute standups
description: Conduct a ceremony effectiveness audit for a team whose daily standups have expanded from 15 to 45 minutes, identify root causes, and recommend corrective actions.
input: Our daily standup has ballooned to 45 minutes. It was 15 minutes 3 months ago. The team is 8 people. People are solving problems during standup, giving detailed status reports, and the PO keeps asking clarifying questions that turn into mini-planning sessions. Team morale around standups is low. Some people have started skipping.
expected_behavior: Identifies multiple root causes: problem-solving in standup, status reporting instead of sync, PO derailing with questions, scope creep into planning
Quantifies the impact: 30 min/day x 8 people = 20 person-hours/week wasted
Recommends specific corrective actions: parking lot for deep discussions, after-standup huddles, PO question protocol
Provides a redesigned standup agenda template with strict 15-min timebox
Includes facilitation scripts for redirecting off-topic discussions
Proposes metrics to track recovery: duration trending, skip rate reduction
Suggests a 2-week experiment with clear success criteria
Addresses the skip rate as a symptom requiring trust rebuilding
